question: If Dog extends Animal, which assignment is safe?

options.text: Producer<Dog> -> Producer<Animal> (covariance in output position)
Producer<Animal> -> Producer<Dog> (covariance works both ways)
options.explanation: Covariance only works from subtype to supertype. Producer<Animal> might return an Animal without breed, violating Producer<Dog> expectations

options.text: Consumer<Dog> -> Consumer<Animal> (contravariance in input position)
options.explanation: Contravariance inverts direction: Consumer<Animal> -> Consumer<Dog>, not the other way. Consumer<Dog> uses dog.breed, and Animal doesn't have that field

options.text: Box<Dog> -> Box<Animal> (read+write are compatible)
options.explanation: Box<T> with get() and set() is invariant — T is used in both input and output positions, so assignment is impossible in either direction

question: Why does TypeScript allow method syntax to be bivariant?

options.text: For compatibility with DOM API, where addEventListener accepts handlers for Event subtypes
Because methods are always safe in both directions
options.explanation: Method bivariance is NOT safe — it allows unsound assignments. This is a deliberate trade-off for compatibility

options.text: Method syntax and property syntax compile to identical code
options.explanation: Both syntaxes compile identically at runtime, but TypeScript checks types differently for them

options.text: Bivariance is a TypeScript bug that they plan to fix
options.explanation: This is a deliberate design decision. strictFunctionTypes makes property syntax strict, but method syntax remains bivariant intentionally

question: Which pattern provides maximum flexibility when working with generic collections?

options.text: Split into ReadRepo<out T> (covariant) and WriteRepo<in T> (contravariant)
Use a single Repository<T> with get() and save() — it's automatically covariant
options.explanation: Repository<T> with read and write methods is invariant because T is used in both input and output positions simultaneously

options.text: Always use any instead of generic parameters
options.explanation: any disables type checking entirely, losing all TypeScript benefits. Variance is solved through interface design

options.text: Make all generic types bivariant through method syntax
options.explanation: Bivariance is unsound. It allows unsafe assignments. Proper read/write interface splitting is safer

question: What do the in and out annotations do in TypeScript 4.7+?

options.text: out T declares T as covariant — T can only be used in output (return) positions
in T declares T as contravariant — T can only be used in input (parameter) positions
TypeScript reports an error if T is used in a position incompatible with its declared variance
in and out change the runtime behavior of generic types
options.explanation: in/out annotations are purely compile-time checks. They don't affect the generated JavaScript code

options.text: Without these annotations TypeScript cannot determine variance
options.explanation: TypeScript determines variance automatically from how T is used. in/out annotations are additional explicit checks and documentation

question: Type Mapper<A, B> = (a: A) => B. What is the variance of A and B?

options.text: A is contravariant (input), B is covariant (output)
Both are covariant because they are generic parameters
options.explanation: Variance depends not on being generic but on usage position. A in parameter position is contravariant, B in return position is covariant

options.text: Both are invariant because they are used in a function
options.explanation: Invariance occurs when one parameter is used in both input and output. Here A is only in input, B only in output

options.text: A is covariant (input), B is contravariant (output)
options.explanation: It's the opposite: input position (parameters) is contravariant, output position (return) is covariant
